**LEDBAT**

**Aim** : To minimize the impact of background traffic on the latency and bandwidth of other connections, by responding early to congestion.

**Motivation**  
Why Ledbat is used in network instead of standard tcp congestion control algorithms?

* Standard TCP treats all traffic equally, hence bandwidth is shared equally among all flows  
* Background applications aim to utilize available bandwidth as efficiently as possible without affecting interactive traffic.  
* LEDBAT enables background applications to yield quickly when competing with interactive flows at a bottleneck link.


**Constraints (as mentioned in RFC)**

* LEDBAT is expected to work well when the sender and/or receiver is connected via a residential access network.  
* Applications should be capable of carrying timestamp and providing frequent acknowledgements




**Algorithm**

**Receiver Side Operation**

On receiving a packet, receiver operates as follows: 

* The receiver extracts the timestamp inserted by the sender  
*  It then computes the one-way delay by subtracting this timestamp from its local reception time.   
* This delay information is included in the acknowledgment packet, which is sent back to the sender along with other required control information.  
  


**Sender side operation**

Variables:

1. TARGET : Maximum queueing delay that LEDBAT may introduce in the network  
2. GAIN : Determines the rate at which cwnd responds to changes in queueing delay. Can be set to a value between 0 and 1\.  
3. off\_target : measures the difference between the measured current queueing delay and the predetermined TARGET delay.  
4. current\_delay : Delay as mentioned in the ACK packet sent by receiver  
5. base\_delay : Minimum one-way delay in the network ( Algorithm assumes this as          minimum queueing delay experienced in the network)  
6. queuing\_delay : current\_delay \- base\_delay  
7. cwnd ; Number of packets that can be sent without waiting for an ACK

**LEDBAT++**

**Aim:** To improve and fix the shortcomings of the original LEDBAT congestion control

Limitations of LEDBAT:

* Latecomer Advantage   
* Inter-LEDBAT fairness   
* Latency drift   
* Low-latency competition   
* Dependency on one-way delay measurements


Modifications introduced in LEDBAT++

Dynamic GAIN

* When the queuing delay is below the target, LEDBAT increases its congestion window in the same way as standard TCP.  
* LEDBAT uses a GAIN parameter (between 0 and 1\) to control how aggressive this increase is.  
* Due to smaller GAIN, LEDBAT increases its cwnd slower than standard TCP connections. Hence LEDBAT achieves smaller throughput.  
* Small GAIN values work well on low-latency paths, where LEDBAT should quickly give way to interactive or foreground traffic.  
* However, on long-delay paths, a small GAIN causes LEDBAT to increase its window too slowly, leaving available bandwidth unused when there is no competing traffic.  
* **LEDBAT++ makes gain dynamic**  
* The GAIN value is computed from the ratio of base delay to target delay, as follows  
    
    
    
  


  
Multiplicative Decrease

* When delay \< TARGET,

  W \+= GAIN   

    

* When delay \> TARGET,

  W \+= max( (GAIN – Constant \* W \* (delay/target \- 1)), \-W/2) )

* This combination of additive increase and multiplicative decrease helps solve the inter-LEDBAT fairness problem

Modified Slow-Start

* LEDBAT++ applies the dynamic gain parameter to increase the congestion windows during the slow start phase  
* LEDBAT++ sender increase cwnd as follows,

  Cwnd \+= amount\_of\_bytes\_acknowledged \* GAIN

* Initial cwnd is restricted to 2 packets  
* In low-latency links, GAIN is less. This ensures that LEDBAT++ ramps up more slowly than standard TCP

LEDBAT Linux Kernal module  
[https://github.com/silviov/TCP-LEDBAT/blob/master/src/tcp\_ledbat.c\#L487](https://github.com/silviov/TCP-LEDBAT/blob/master/src/tcp_ledbat.c#L487)

**FreeBSD Related** 

Directory with congestion control protocols  
/usr/src/sys/netinet/cc/

(During freeBSD setup, ensure the src option is selected for download, else the directory will appear empty)

Source code tree   
Link 1 \- Congestion control  
[https://www.leidinger.net/FreeBSD/dox/netinet/html/df/de5/cc\_\_cubic\_8c\_source.html](https://www.leidinger.net/FreeBSD/dox/netinet/html/df/de5/cc__cubic_8c_source.html)  
Link 2  
[https://cgit.freebsd.org/src/tree/sys/netinet/cc/cc\_cubic.c](https://cgit.freebsd.org/src/tree/sys/netinet/cc/cc_cubic.c)

![][image1]

Sysctl in freeBSD   
The last few lines of the newreno / vegas in freeBsd use sysctl to expose parameters to tune  
Might be useful to read up \-   
[https://freebsdfoundation.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/01/Implementing-System-Control-Nodes-sysctl.pdf](https://freebsdfoundation.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/01/Implementing-System-Control-Nodes-sysctl.pdf)

**Major Project** 

***Design and Evaluation of TCP LEDBAT, LEDBAT++ in FreeBSD Under Mixed Traffic and Queue Disciplines***

Proposed Methodologies for LEDBAT implementation

**1\. Kernel-Level Integration (FreeBSD Implementation)**

This method involves implementing LEDBAT as a congestion control module within the FreeBSD kernel-space.

**2\. User-Space Implementation**

This method puts the LEDBAT logic inside the program itself rather than the operating system. The main advantage is that the code is portable—it can run on Windows, Mac, or Linux without needing special administrator "root" access. It is also safer to develop because a mistake in the code won't crash the entire computer.

This can be implemented using two different types of sockets:

* UDP Sockets: The application sends data via UDP and must manually handle reliability, sequencing, and acknowledgments to calculate the delays required for LEDBAT.  
    
* TCP Sockets: The application sends data via TCP and monitors the connection's delay to slow down the data flow when the network gets crowded.

Reference: [https://github.com/bittorrent/libutp](https://github.com/bittorrent/libutp)

**Deliverables**  
1.FreeBSD LEDBAT CC Module  
	Pluggable via [net.inet.tcp.cc](http://net.inet.tcp.cc).algorithm=ledbat

2\. Network Testbed  
FreeBSD VNET / Jails

\[ Sender A \] \----\\  
                  \[ Router \] \---- \[ Receiver \]  
\[ Sender B \] \----/

| Flow Type | Tool | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| TCP LEDBAT | iperf3 | Background traffic |
| TCP CUBIC / Reno | iperf3 | Foreground bulk |
| UDP CBR | iperf3 / custom | VoIP / streaming |
| UDP Bursty | custom script | Gaming / realtime |

Router may be experimented against different qdisc to observe variation in metric. Other topologies like full-dumbbell, etc. may also be used

### 

### **Metrics**

One-way delay (OWD), RTT inflation, Queue occupancy, throughput share, jain’s fairness index, packet loss rate
